posts.name: JR George
posts.cooked:
Hi All,

I’m looking for a 1980 birth year wine. I’ve heard that Napa and Rhône are the better options that year. How/where would I begin to find where to purchase? Any help and suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks!

posts.name: Alex Nichols
posts.cooked: State your budget for a 750ml bottle or how much total you want to spend and the suggestions will start pouring in.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: If you’re open to Port, Dow is quite good. Maybe others too.

posts.name: JR George
posts.cooked: Thanks Alex! I’d say up to $350. If absolutely amazing, I could go a little higher.

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked: My spouse is a 1980 vintage. I suggest Yquem

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
You rang? 1980 here as well and I’ve drank/stocked up on quite a few. Highlights for me have been CA based - cabs, Petite Sirah, some zins, and a killer Heitz chardonnay. Good news is that with it being a ‘lesser’ vintage, you can snag most for reasonable prices (if/when you find them). Standouts include

-Heitz Chardonnay (unbelievably color and still fresh. Still have 1 left along with a Montelena that I’m excited about
-Nevada City Winery Zinfandel (unheard of winery but it was incredible)
-Ridge Geyserville
-Ridge Zin Howell Mountain
-BV Georges de Latour (killer out of magnum but great out of standard bottles as well)
-Niebaum-Coppola Rubicon from magnum
-Carneros Creek Cabernet
-Wente Vineyards Petite Sirah Library, Livermore Valley
-Inglenook Petite Sirah

Wines I’m looking forward to:
-Ridge Petite Sirah York Creek, Devil’s Hill
-Spring Mountain Cab (magnum)
-Mouton Rothschild
-Heitz Bella Oaks
-Villa Mt. Eden Reserve Cab
-Silver Oak Alexander Valley
-Buena Vista Pinot Noir
-Chateau Climens
-Chateau Rieussec
-Dom Perignon
-Moet Brut Imperial
-Vueve Clicquot Ponsardin
-Beringer Port
-Beringer Nightingale

Looking forward to hearing what you end up trying!

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
My spouse is a 1980 vintage. I suggest Yquem

+1. Also, the '80 Climens is lovely at a fraction of the price. You could get a bottle of classic napa cab (1980 Heitz Martha’s or Ridge MB maybe) AND the climens?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s a very good red Burgundy vintage! A friend and I share a birthday, but he was born in 1980, so in a few days we are celebrating together around the 1980 theme. I’m also working on a 40 years on retrospective of the vintage in Burgundy as it happens. Of course, not easy to find, nor inexpensive sadly…

posts.name: Martin Petersen
posts.cooked: Drank a bottle of Roagna Barbaresco 1980 last night and it was drinking beautifully right now

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Yes again on burgundy. The few I’ve had have all been winners. Voillot Champans was sensational I had a few years back.

posts.name: JR George
posts.cooked: Where would I go about finding some of these? I’m usually buying wines from the vineyards or in the store?

posts.name: Scott Jameson
posts.cooked:
Winebid has a number of 1980 CA Cabs (and other 1980 wines) this week. Some of the valuations seem strong compared to historical prices while others don’t seem too bad.

Since you are in TX, you’re somewhat limited as to possible sources due to state laws prohibiting out-of-state retailers from shipping into TX.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
My spouse is a 1980 vintage. I suggest Yquem

Fabulous wine

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
It’s a very good red Burgundy vintage! A friend and I share a birthday, but he was born in 1980, so in a few days we are celebrating together around the 1980 theme. I’m also working on a 40 years on retrospective of the vintage in Burgundy as it happens. Of course, not easy to find, nor inexpensive sadly…

Interesting. I enjoyed a great many 1980 Burgs, a very enjoyable and underrated vintage, but have not had one in a long time. I would not have expected them to have lived this long. Neat that they are doing so well.

posts.name: G. Keeler
posts.cooked: My wife was born in 80 as well. 1980 Lopez de Heredia Rioja Gran Reserva Tondonia was one of the better 80’s we’ve tried. It helps that it’s one of her favorite producers as well.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
Winebid has a number of 1980 CA Cabs (and other 1980 wines) this week. Some of the valuations seem strong compared to historical prices while others don’t seem too bad.

Since you are in TX, you’re somewhat limited as to possible sources due to state laws prohibiting out-of-state retailers from shipping into TX.

Yep, totally agree that the prices are high this week vs. What you normally find, particularly on the cabs.

I’d hone in on the bottles you want and just use wine-searcher pro to find them (believe you can filter by retailers that ship to your area)

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
My wife was born in 80 as well. 1980 Lopez de Heredia Rioja Gran Reserva Tondonia was one of the better 80’s we’ve tried. It helps that it’s one of her favorite producers as well.

Nice! That is one that I have not tried/found yet, but definitley hope to at some point!

posts.name: JR George
posts.cooked: Thanks y’all! I joined this site because it looked like y’all were helpful. Y’all are wonderful.

posts.name: JR George
posts.cooked:
Definitely doing a little more research and joined wine-searcher. Found a local small wine shop, going to try them before I order anything.

Now… the next question will be a 1982 vintage!!

posts.name: Joshua Roby
posts.cooked:
1982 is all about Bordeaux in my limited experience.

I’ve been searching for ‘80’s for a years. Gotten much more expensive the last few months. Bunch of people turning 40 this year, have time to hunt for them and they aren’t taking celebratory trips is my take.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
1982 is all about Bordeaux in my limited experience.

I’ve been searching for ‘80’s for a years. Gotten much more expensive the last few months. Bunch of people turning 40 this year, have time to hunt for them and they aren’t taking celebratory trips is my take.

I think you’re spot on - definitley seen the prices creep up recently. Still deals to be had vs. My wife’s birth year of 78. Really have to pay to play with that vintage.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: DRC did great in 1980. I had RC and LT, one bottle of each, each purchased for $100, and loved them. Those were the days!

posts.name: Jonathan Favre
posts.cooked: I’ve had some really nice bottles of the Guigal Landonne not so long ago - think I have 1-2 somewhere… Vietti believe it or not can be good also. The '80 Caymus SS is a sturdy wine for sure - should be fun to try if you can find it.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I was looking through my inventory, and there were a few weird ones. My beloved Sobrero Barolo, some Chevillon including a Denis Chevillon, and the best one, a Penfolds Bin 80a, a serious rarity.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
DRC did great in 1980. I had RC and LT, one bottle of each, each purchased for $100, and loved them. Those were the days!

The way I rationalize it is that DRC are really missing out for not having hot, young consumers like us today

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: Dow’s Vintage Port is great. One of the best of the decade. All of the Symington brands did well, Grahams, Warres, Smith Woodhouse, Gould Campbell. Still inexpensive. Taylor, Fladgate brands were less impressive with the exception of Fonseca.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
Dow’s Vintage Port is great. One of the best of the decade. All of the Symington brands did well, Grahams, Warres, Smith Woodhouse, Gould Campbell. Still inexpensive. Taylor, Fladgate brands were less impressive with the exception of Fonseca.

Love hearing that! I have most of those but have not opened one yet. Would you say they’re ready to go, or ideally let em rest a little longer? Thanks!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
DRC did great in 1980. I had RC and LT, one bottle of each, each purchased for $100, and loved them. Those were the days!

Bought the DRC Grands Echezeaux for $35. Fabulous wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
DRC did great in 1980. I had RC and LT, one bottle of each, each purchased for $100, and loved them. Those were the days!

Bought the DRC Grands Echezeaux for $35. Fabulous wine.

We’re having that on Thursday! Alongside Ponsot CdlR and Georges Mugneret Ruchottes. Quite excited, I never had the '80 GE before…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’ve had some really nice bottles of the Guigal Landonne not so long ago - think I have 1-2 somewhere…

Yes, and it’s actually ready to drink!

Southern Rhônes can be a nice surprise, Fortia Tête de Cru is nice and still full of fresh fruit, albeit not on the level of the heights that cuvée hit in the 1970s.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
1982 is all about Bordeaux in my limited experience.

Also great for white Burgundy (maybe the best ever Leflaive vintage, rivaled only by 1979 for me) and very good indeed for Champagne.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
DRC did great in 1980. I had RC and LT, one bottle of each, each purchased for $100, and loved them. Those were the days!

Bought the DRC Grands Echezeaux for $35. Fabulous wine.

We’re having that on Thursday! Alongside Ponsot CdlR and Georges Mugneret Ruchottes. Quite excited, I never had the '80 GE before…

It has been a very, very long time since I had this wine ($35 seems unbelievably expensive in the early 1980s) but it was truly fabulous back then. I hope that it has continued to be great and that you love it.

Here is a fun idea on the birth year theme: go with the Chinese zodiac years. In your case, you are Year of the Monkey. So that means 1968, 1980 (suggestions above), 1992 (white burg stands out in my mind), 2004 (champagne?), 2016 (Côte de Nuits reds).

This concept can make for a fun dinner with wines at various states of maturity.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
Bought the DRC Grands Echezeaux for $35. Fabulous wine.

We’re having that on Thursday! Alongside Ponsot CdlR and Georges Mugneret Ruchottes. Quite excited, I never had the '80 GE before…

It has been a very, very long time since I had this wine ($35 seems unbelievably expensive in the early 1980s) but it was truly fabulous back then. I hope that it has continued to be great and that you love it.

Adjusted for inflation that would be $110 today. I understand that the standards for what was expensive or not back then were much different.

And plenty of safe bets in Napa in 1980.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
And plenty of safe bets in Napa in 1980.

I remember liking some 1980 California wines, esp. BV Private Reserve and Jordan, but have no idea what has held up. I don’t think I have ever had the 1980 Montelena (have danced around it having 1978, 1982, 1984, etc.) but not the 1982. What 1980s are still good today?

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
And plenty of safe bets in Napa in 1980.

I remember liking some 1980 California wines, esp. BV Private Reserve and Jordan, but have no idea what has held up. I don’t think I have ever had the 1980 Montelena (have danced around it having 1978, 1982, 1984, etc.) but not the 1982. What 1980s are still good today?

I’ll copy my list from up thread - not all napa, but from CA. Sound bottles of BV Georges are still great- especially out of magnum. In addition to the below, Duckhorn cab a few years back was still solid, and a Montevina cab a few months back was decent (out of a rough bottle with seepage), but tasted more like an aged zin (maybe more because of the producer and Amador fruit?)

-Heitz Chardonnay (unbelievably color and still fresh. Still have 1 left along with a Montelena that I’m excited about
-Nevada City Winery Zinfandel (unheard of winery but it was incredible)
-Ridge Geyserville
-Ridge Zin Howell Mountain
-BV Georges de Latour (killer out of magnum but great out of standard bottles as well)
-Niebaum-Coppola Rubicon from magnum
-Carneros Creek Cabernet
-Wente Vineyards Petite Sirah Library, Livermore Valley
-Inglenook Petite Sirah

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked:
Red Burgs (if you can find them) as mentioned have been really lovely. I’ve also had success with some Napa Cabs like Diamond Creek and some other classics. This past may I opened a Cappellano for my 40th and it was killer. I’ve also come across some other Piedmont wines from 80 and have been pretty happy with them.

Of course, I do like to joke that 78 and 82 average out to 80 and that allows for some more options
